step2_rag_chunks: If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.

step2_rag_chunks: When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
Sleep problems may occur after quitting smoking, but these usually improve after a few weeks.

step2_rag_chunks: After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.

- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 4 — Planning]

You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.
[Patient Profile]
- Smoking status: a pack a day
- Quit goal: quit smoking
- Motivation to quit: stress at work, health issues, health risks, family pressure
- Known triggers: stress, morning craving
- Tried strategies: quit before → never lasts
[Clinical Knowledge]

- After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
- If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
- Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
[Recent Conversation]

Therapist: Okay, that helps me understand your level of nicotine dependence. There are many nicotine replacement therapy options available that can help manage cravings and withdrawal symptoms. We could discuss those further if you're interested. Additionally, there are prescription medications that can help reduce cravings and make quitting easier. Would you like to learn more about those options?
Patient: Yeah, that would be helpful.
Therapist: Great! Now, let's talk about your social support system. Do you have friends or family members who could support you in your quit journey?
Patient: Yeah, my sister has been trying to get me to quit for years. She's always been really supportive.

Therapist: That's great! Having a supportive network can make a big difference in your success. I'd encourage you to involve your sister in your quit plan and ask for her support. We can also discuss ways to build a stronger support system for you.
Patient: Okay, that sounds like a good idea.
